mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the major device categories in outline B: internal combustion engines (B.8.a) and steam engines (B.4.a) are both explicitly listed under devices for utilizing chemical and strain/compression energy, and the article treats the full taxonomy of energy-to-mechanical-work conversion.

Exactly the topic named at B.8.a — gasoline, diesel, jet, turbojet, fan-jet, turboprop, and rocket engines are all internal combustion types covered in the article, matching the outline's explicit enumeration.

The outline at B.5.b names induction motors, synchronous motors, and commutator motors utilizing AC and DC — precisely the taxonomy the article covers under devices for utilizing magnetic and electrical energy.

C.4 explicitly lists batteries and fuel cells as energy-conversion devices; the article covers the electrochemical cell principle, primary vs. rechargeable types, and energy storage — direct coverage of this path.

D.2 names transistors, semiconductor diodes, and integrated circuits; D.3 names liquid-crystal displays, optical fibres, and semiconductor lasers — all treated in this article on semiconductor physics and devices.

The article covers transistors, diodes, and integrated circuits (D.2) and the semiconductor basis of optoelectronic devices (D.3), directly matching both solid-state and optoelectronic device categories in outline section D.

Tesla's AC induction motor design is the canonical device for B.5.b, and his polyphase AC power system underpins turbine-driven generator infrastructure (C.7); the article covers both contributions substantively.
B.8.b explicitly names black powder as the first entry under chemical explosives; this article is the primary source on gunpowder's composition, history, and use as a low explosive and propellant.
B.4.a directly names steam engines and steam power plants; the article covers Savery, Newcomen, and Watt designs, configurations, and the Rankine cycle — exact coverage of this outline path.

Covers electromagnetic induction and power generation principles underlying B.5 (magnetic and electrical energy devices) and C.7 (electric power generation), connecting foundational physics to the technology of electrical energy utilization.
A.3 lists nonrenewable energy sources including oil; the article treats petroleum's formation, extraction, and refining into fuels, covering oil as a primary nonrenewable energy source.

A.1 includes nuclear fission and radioactivity among primary energy sources; B.9 names nuclear fission reactors, radioactive isotopes, and nuclear fusion reactors — all directly treated in this article on civilian nuclear power generation.

A.2 lists water energy among recurring sources; B.2 names water turbines; C.7 explicitly lists hydraulic-turbine-driven generators — the article covers all three aspects including pumped storage and dispatchability.
Plasma is the medium for thermonuclear fusion reactions (A.1) and is the working fluid in magnetohydrodynamic power generators (C.3), which the article addresses through MHD and fusion contexts.
Silicon is the dominant material for the transistors, diodes, and ICs of D.2 and for semiconductor lasers and solar cell photovoltaics of D.3; the article explicitly covers both application domains.

C.5 names lamps and other lighting devices as energy-conversion devices; the article covers all three lamp categories (incandescent, discharge, LED) as electrical-to-light converters — direct coverage of this path.
B.8.a explicitly lists rocket engines among internal combustion engine types; the article covers liquid, solid, monopropellant, and hybrid rocket propulsion in detail.

D.3 names semiconductor lasers among optoelectronic devices; the article covers semiconductor, gas, and solid-state laser types and their optoelectronic basis, directly fitting the optoelectronic device category.

B.8.b lists nitroglycerin, dynamites, nitrocellulosic explosives, and military explosives among chemical explosives; this article covers the full taxonomy of high and low explosives, sensitivity classification, and detonation properties.
A.3 explicitly lists natural gas as a nonrenewable energy source; the article covers methane composition, extraction, and use in power generation and heating.

A.2 lists solar energy among recurring energy sources; B.10 names devices for utilizing solar energy — the article covers photovoltaic systems, concentrated solar power, and passive solar techniques, matching both paths.

B.7 explicitly names refrigeration equipment alongside heat exchangers under devices for utilizing heat energy; the article covers vapor-compression, absorption, thermoelectric, and magnetic refrigeration cycles in full detail.

Section A surveys major types of energy useful to humankind; this article provides the foundational definitions and taxonomy of all energy forms (chemical, thermal, nuclear, radiant, kinetic, potential) that underpin all of section A's categories.
A.3 lists coal, natural gas, and oil as nonrenewable energy sources; the article covers all three fossil fuels together, their formation, and their dominance as primary energy sources.

A.1 includes radioactivity among primary energy sources; B.9 mentions radioactive isotopes as devices for utilizing nuclear energy — the article covers decay modes, half-lives, and applications including nuclear energy production and medical isotopes.

A.2 includes wind energy among recurring sources; B.2 names windmills and wind turbines as devices for utilizing wind energy — the article covers wind physics, generation mechanisms, and power generation applications.
A.3 explicitly names coal as a nonrenewable energy source; the article covers coal grades, energy content, and its role as the largest single source in global electricity generation.

B.1 names the pulley, lever, and block and tackle among devices for utilizing muscle energy; simple machines are the direct topic, covering all six classical devices including pulley and treadmill referenced in the outline.

B.2 covers water turbines and hydropower infrastructure; C.7 lists hydraulic-turbine-driven generators — dams are the enabling structures for both, and the article explicitly covers hydroelectric power generation.

Electron tubes (D.1) and solid-state devices (D.2) both depend on electron behavior; the article covers free electrons enabling cathode ray tubes (D.1) and carrier electrons in semiconductor devices (D.2).
